phase_prompt.objective: Implement correct duration stability calculation that measures per-test consistency across runs, rather than uniformity of test durations within a run.

problem.issue: This measured variance between different tests (2s test vs 60s test = high CV), not stability of each test across multiple runs.

solution.description: Calculate coefficient of variation (CV) for each test across its runs, then average all test CVs for overall stability.

sql_approach.cte: Group by test_name + file, calculate STDDEV/AVG per test, only include tests with COUNT > 1 (multiple runs)
Use subquery to get AVG(cv) from per_test_cv CTE

solution.example: Test A runs: [2s, 2.1s, 1.9s] -> CV ≈ 0.05 (stable) Test B runs: [60s, 61s, 59s] -> CV ≈ 0.017 (stable) Average CV = ~0.034 -> 97% stability

changes.change: Added current_per_test_cv CTE with GROUP BY test_name, file
Added previous_per_test_cv CTE for trend calculation
Modified current_metrics to use subquery for duration_cv
Modified previous_metrics to use subquery for duration_cv
Added HAVING COUNT(*) > 1 to exclude single-run tests

edge_cases.case: Tests with only 1 run are excluded from CV calculation (HAVING COUNT > 1)
If no tests have multiple runs, AVG returns NULL, COALESCE returns 0 = 100% stable
Tests with 0 average duration return CV = 0 (CASE WHEN avg > 0)

result.notes: Multi-run stability now correctly reflects actual test duration consistency. Different tests having different natural durations no longer affects stability.
